videos.title: How to spend 4 days in Rio de Janeiro? - Travel Guide
videos.description:
How to spend 4 days in Rio de Janeiro?
Welcome to the heart of Brazil! Dive deep into the wonders of Rio de Janeiro with us over four unforgettable days. From the lively vibes of Copacabana Beach to the soul-stirring views atop Sugarloaf Mountain, Rio promises a feast for the senses. Here's what you can expect:

🏖️ Day 1: Bask in the golden hues of Copacabana Beach and discover the rhythms of Ipanema Beach.
🌅 Day 2: Marvel at the monumental Christ the Redeemer and ascend the majestic Sugarloaf Mountain.
🎨 Day 3: Revel in the artistic splendor of the Lapa Arches and iconic Selaron Steps.
🍃 Day 4: Experience tranquility at the Jardim Botânico and enjoy the serene beauty of Lagoa Rodrigo de Freitas.

Tantalize your taste buds with traditional Brazilian dishes, unravel tales hidden in the city's architecture, and immerse yourself in a fusion of history, art, and nature. Join us as we unveil the essence of Rio de Janeiro, a city that dances to the beat of its own drum. Don't just witness, experience Rio with us! 🇧🇷✈️
videos.transcript: Welcome to Rio De Janeiro join us in exploration of this vibrant City where each day is a celebration of life itself from the iconic Copa Cabana Beach to The Majestic Christ the Redeemer statue we'll explore the known and hidden gems and cultural Treasures that make Rio [Music] unique your adventure begins on the world famous Copa Cabana Beach a 4 km long stretch of golden sand that has hosted numerous events including the annual New Year's Eve fireworks Extravaganza also did you know that Copa Cabana was named after a small image of the Virgin Mary called the Copa Cabana Madonna it's a beach where cultures music and sports unite also don't forget to explore the historic Copa Cabana Fort built in 1914 which has played a significant role in protecting Rio's Coastline it now houses the Army Historical Museum enjoy sweeping View views of the beach and the vast Atlantic Ocean for lunch dive into a traditional Brazilian dish called Bolino debaca delicious codfish fritters that are a local favorite as the sun begins to set head to iima beach another iconic Rio destination this beach is not just a place it's a state of mind it was the inspiration behind the legendary bosan NOA song the girl from Epona take a stroll along the iconic Copa Cabana and EPA boardwalks known as kidone and learn about the rich musical Heritage that emerged from these [Music] Shores get ready for a day of breathtaking views and spiritual reflection your first stop is the awe inspiring Christ the Redeemer statue a symbol of Rio and Christianity worldwide it stands 30 m tall at Top corvado Mountain offering a panoramic view of the city but did you know that this colossal statue created by French sculptor Paul lowski was constructed using 6 million stone tiles in concrete After experiencing the spiritual significance of Christ the Redeemer indulge in a traditional Brazilian fada for lunch it's a hearty black bean stew that represents the fusion of African Portuguese and Indigenous flavors in the afternoon take a cable car ride to Sugarloaf Mountain this iconic Granite Peak Rising 396 m above the harbor has been captivating visitors since 19112 it provides 360° on day three you'll immerse yourself in Rio's artistic and cultural gems start at the lapa Arches a historic Aqueduct dating back to the 18th century originally built to supply water to the city it's now an iconic structure that hosts the Santa Teresa tram known as bondinho this area comes alive at night with Samba and Lively nightlife just steps away you'll find the colorful ceron steps an freshwater Lagoon is connected to the Sea by a canal and offers recre recreational activities like standup paddle boarding cycling and bird watching did you know that during the 2016 Rio Olympics the Lagoon hosted the rowing and canoeing events making it a global stage for sports explore each day's unique experiences and immerse yourself in the captivating culture history and natural beauty of Rio videos.transcript: Rio de Janeiro is a city that needs no introduction known for its stunning beaches vibrant nightlife and iconic landmarks it's a destination that should be on everyone's bucket list here are some of the top things to do in Rio de Janeiro number 24 hang out with Jesus Christ Cristo Redentor or Christ the Redeemer is a massive statue of Jesus Christ located on top of coracovado mountain standing at over 98 feet tall this is the most famous Christ statue in the world from its perch on the mountain the statue offers panoramic views of the city and the surrounding landscape the views from the top of corticovado mountain are truly breathtaking you can see the city of Rio De Janeiro spread out below you as well as the surrounding mountains and forests on a clear day you might even be able to see the Atlantic Ocean in the distance but the views from the top of coracovado mountain aren't the only reason to visit the journey to the top is an adventure in itself you can take a train up the mountain or you can hike to the Top If you're feeling adventurous either way the journey will take you through the lust tejuka Forest where you'll have the opportunity to see some amazing wildlife and plants number 23 explore tijuka National Park if you're a nature lover you can't miss tijuka National Park this is the largest urban rainforest in the world and is home to a staggering array of plant and animal life it also provides amazing hiking and rock climbing opportunities hike through the dense jungle and you might spot monkeys parrots and even the occasional foxes it's also home to several waterfalls and rivers making it a great place for a swim on a hot day whether you're a seasonal hiker or just looking for a leisurely stroll there's something for everyone in tichua National Park number 22 cable car ride up Sugarloaf Mountain Sugarloaf Mountain is an iconic landmark in Rio de Janeiro it gets its name from its distinctive shape which is said to resemble the traditional shape of Sugar Loaf molds used to mold sugar in the past visitors can take a cable car to the top of Sugarloaf Mountain which takes approximately three minutes to reach the first stop at Moro da urica and another three minutes to reach the top of the Sugarloaf proper the cable car ride offers breathtaking views of the city and the top of the mountain feature is a viewing platform where visitors can take in the 360 degree panoramic view of Rio De Janeiro the mountain has been featured in numerous films and television shows and it is a popular spot for romantic proposals and wedding photos number 21 take a selfie at Celeron steps the stairs of escaria Celeron are absolutely stunning with over 2 000 brightly colored tiles from all over the world covering every inch of the surface it's like walking through a giant Mosaic your Instagram feed and your inner artist will thank you but it's not just about the pretty pictures the stairs have an interesting backstory too they were created by Chilean artist Jorge Celeron as a tribute to the Brazilian people and he spent over 20 years collecting and installing tiles from around the globe it's a true labor of love and it shows in every intricate detail these famous steps have been featured in many magazines documentaries and even music videos number 20 Copacabana and Ipanema beaches Copacabana and Ipanema are 2 who are the most famous and iconic beaches in Rio de Janeiro the popularity of Copacabana can be traced back to the 1940s when it was a popular destination for movie stars and celebrities the beach is known for its wide crescent-shaped Beach and iconic Promenade which stretches over 2.5 miles and is lined with palm trees bars and shops you'll be able to witness the biggest party on the planet over New Year's Eve when more than 2 million people gather on the beach to welcome the New Year Ipanema on the other hand became known to the world in the 1960s through the popular Bossa Nova song The Girl From Ipanema which introduced its Allure to a wider audience the beach is known for its beautiful stretch of white sand and crystal clear waters and you'll see many local swimming surfing and playing beach volleyball Ipanema is also known for its upscale shopping and dining and it's a popular spot for people watching and soaking up the local culture number 19. hang gliding above Rio Rio de Janeiro is known for its breathtaking Landscapes and hang gliding a perfect way to take it all in Imagine soaring above the city's iconic beaches mountains and favelas getting an unrivaled bird's eye view of everything the city has to offer it's an experience you won't find anywhere else you'll be accompanied by an experienced instructor who will guide you every step of the way and make sure you have a safe and enjoyable flight and even if you're a bit nervous at first don't worry the sensation of flying through the air is totally exhilarating and will leave you feeling on top of the world number 18 chill out at parakeet parakeet is a lovely park known for its stunning Gardens romantic gazebos and delightful backdrop including the iconic corticovado mountain and Christ the Redeemer statue the park is home to a photogenic Mansion which was once the residence of the Brazilian art Patron Enrique larger and his wife and now serves as an art school and Cultural Center and is a relatively under visited tourist destination harike laja is a popular spot for picnics leisurely walks and his has been featured as a location in various media including music videos some of the music videos that have been filmed at parakeet laja include those by Snoop Dogg Pharrell Williams and the Black Eyed Peas number 17. take in the street art in lapa the street art in lapa is absolutely stunning with vibrant murals and graffiti covering every inch of the neighborhood it's like walking through an open air museum with each turn revealing a new and exciting piece of art plus it's located in the heart of Rio De Janeiro so you can take in all the vibrant culture and nightlife while you're there but it's not just about the pretty pictures the street art in lapa is also a window into the city's Rich cultural history and Contemporary Art scene many of the murals and graffiti pieces are inspired by Brazilian culture politics and social issues and they provide a unique insight into the city's identity and perspective number 16 visit the fascinating Metropolitan Cathedral the Metropolitan Cathedral of Saint Sebastian is a modernist Masterpiece located in the center of Rio its unique ufo-like design is sure to catch your eye inside the cathedral is just as impressive with soaring ceilings resplendent stained glass windows and a series of chapels dedicated to various Saints it's the seat of the Archbishop of Rio De Janeiro and has been an important center of Catholicism in the city for decades number 15 discover the most beautiful library in the world the Royal Portuguese reading room is an absolutely gorgeous building it houses an incredible collection of books with over 350 000 volumes it's one of the largest and most important libraries in South America you'll find everything from rare and valuable works of Portuguese literature to Modern bestsellers for the real draw of this library is its Grand neoclassical structure with ornate architectural details marble columns and a stunning interior it's no wonder many guidebooks name this the most beautiful library in the world number 14. day trip to Ilia granji iliagrangi is an alluring Island located just off the coast of Rio it's an easy day trip from the city but it feels like a world away the island is known for its unspoiled tropical beaches crystal clear waters and rugged landscape making it a paradise for nature lovers Elio Grande is also a great place to relax and escape the hustle and bustle of city life it's relatively underdeveloped with no cars allowed on the island and a number of small Charming towns and villages to explore the lack of development also means that the island is relatively unspoiled with plenty of natural beauty to enjoy number 13 challenge yourself up the Pedro de gabia the Pedro de gavia is a massive rock formation located in the tiju Forest National Park it stands at almost 2 800 feet tall and offers stunning views of the city and the surrounding Countryside the climb to the top is no easy feat it's a physically demanding and Technical climb that requires a good level of fitness and a little rock climbing experience but the reward is totally worth it the views from the top are absolutely breathtaking and the scent of a comp publishment you'll feel after making it to the top is unbeatable this is the ultimate test of Endurance by the way if you want to learn how to save money on airfares then click the link in the description below this guide will teach you how to always get the cheapest flights number 12 shop at the hippie Market of Ipanema the hippie Market of Ipanema also known as the Fiera de Ipanema is a popular outdoor Market located in the trendy Ipanema neighborhood of Rio it's a lively and colorful Place known for its handmade crafts local art and one-of-a-kind souvenirs and Gifts you'll find everything from jewelry and clothing to home decor and accessories at the market buying crafts here is a great way to support local artists and Artisans the hippie Market is open every Sunday from 9am to 6 p.m and it's a popular spot for both locals and tourists it's also a great place to people watch and soak up the vibrant culture of Rio there are often street performers and food vendors at the market adding to the Lively atmosphere number 11 josinia Favela walking tour a favela is a community of mostly low-income families who have built their homes on the Steep hillsides of the city while living conditions in favelas can be difficult they are also vibrant and Lively neighborhoods with a strong sense of community and pride ossinia is the largest favela in Brazil and has been dubbed a city within a city taking a walking tour is a great way to learn more about the history and culture of these informal settlements and get a glimpse of daily life in the Favela many walking tours of hostenia are led by local guides who are passionate about sharing their Community with visitors they'll take you on a tour of the narrow streets and alleys of the Favela introducing you to local businesses Community organizations and cultural landmarks you'll also have the opportunity to meet and interact with local residents giving you a unique and authentic cultural experience number 10 dance like Michael Jackson at Favela Santa Marta one of the most notable aspects of Favela Santa Marta is that it is the first Favela in Rio to be pacified which means that it has been the target of a government initiative to improve living conditions and reduce crime as a result Favela Santa Marta is considered one of the safest favelas in Rio another notable feature of Favela Santa Marta is its colorful houses the houses are often built with a mix of materials and painted in bright colors adding to the vibrant and Lively atmosphere of the neighborhood this makes it a great spot for taking Instagram photos Favela Santa Marta is also known for being the location where Michael Jackson filmed part of his music video for the song They Don't Care About Us the video which was released in 1996 features Jackson dancing and singing in the streets of Favela Santa Marta highlighting the neighborhood's vibrant culture and energy number nine hang off a cliff at Pedra de telegrapho located a 90-minute drive from Rio Pedro de telegrapho is a popular spot for tourists and locals to visit due to the unique photographs that can be taken here the rock offers a panoramic view of the surrounding landscape and the perspective of the photographs taken from the top can make it appear as though the person in the photo is hanging off a cliff while the photographs are certainly a draw The Rock is also a popular destination for outdoor enthusiasts and anyone looking for a challenging and rewarding hike with picturesque views number eight visit South America's largest aquarium Aqua Rio is the largest aquarium in South America and is located at the heart of Rio De Janeiro it is home to over 8 000 animals representing more than 350 different species from all over the world the aquarium is divided into several different exhibits each showcasing a different type of marine life visitors can see everything from colorful tropical fish and sharks to dolphins sea turtles and even Penguins the aquarium also has a number of interactive exhibits including touch tanks and a simulated dive experience where visitors can get up close and personal with the animals you can even spend a night in the tunnel that passes in the middle of the ocean tank number seven see the largest street art mural in the world located on Olympic Boulevard in Rio the mural was created by Brazilian artist Eduardo Cobra and is truly a sight to behold it's an enormous piece of art covering the entire side of a building and measuring over 12 000 square feet the mural depicts iconic figures from Brazil's history and culture including musician Chico buarque soccer player Pele an indigenous leader kayapo but the mural isn't just a pretty picture it's also a symbol of Hope and unity the Miro was created as a tribute to the 2016 Olympic games which were held in Rio de Janeiro and it celebrates the diversity and unity of the city and its people number six Marvel at the Museum of Tomorrow the Museum of tomorrow is not your average boring old history lesson it's a futuristic interactive experience that will blow your mind the museum encourages visitors to consider the big questions about the future and to think critically about the choices we make today and how they will impact the World of Tomorrow but let's talk about the architecture for a minute the Museum of tomorrow was designed by the world renowned architect Santiago caletrava and is truly a work of art the building itself is shaped like a giant silver boat and it's surrounded by a beautiful reflecting pool inside you'll find a spacious open-air Atrium with a sweeping curved ceiling it's like nothing you've ever seen before but the real show stopper is the rooftop Terrace from up there you'll get an incredible view of the city and the harbor it's the perfect spot to take some Instagram worthy photos and just soak in the sights and sounds of Rio number five ride the historic Santa Teresa tram the Santa Teresa tram is not your average public transportation experience it's a Historic Landmark in itself the tram's been around since the late 1800s and has become an iconic symbol of the city riding it is like stepping back in time and getting a glimpse of what life was like in Rio de Janeiro over a century ago but beyond its historical significance the Santa Teresa tram is just plain fun it's a leisurely ride that takes you through the winding cobblestone streets of the Bohemian Santa Teresa neighborhood along the way you'll get to see some of the city's most captivating colonial style houses and a stop at Charming little squares and Parks you'll also have the opportunity to hop off and explore the neighborhood on foot or just sit back and take in the views as you ride through the hills number four hike to the two brothers Lookout the two brothers Lookout or Moro deuts Hermos is a popular hiking spot for hikers and nature lovers thanks to its beautiful views and relatively easy Trails on a clear day you'll be able to see for miles and miles in every direction the vistas of the city the ocean and the surrounding mountains are truly breathtaking it's the perfect spot for a picnic and some Instagram worthy photos but beyond the views the hike itself is a lot of fun the trail is well marked and not too strenuous making it suitable for hikers of all levels along the way you'll pass through some Splendid Lush vegetation and have the opportunity to see some local Wildlife you might even spot a Capuchin monkey or a toucan number three watch a football match at the Maracana stadium football or soccer is a huge part part of Brazilian culture and the matakana stadium is the mecca of Brazilian football the stadium is an absolute Marvel of engineering and architecture it's one of the largest and most iconic football stadiums in the world with the seating capacity of over 78 thousand just stepping inside the stadium is an awe-inspiring experience but the real fun starts when the match begins watching a football game in Brazil is an electrifying experience especially in the matakana stadium the energy of the crowd is contagious and you'll find yourself cheering and chanting along with the rest of the fans trust me it's an experience you'll never forget and even if you're not a huge football fan the matakana stadium is still worth a visit the atmosphere is electric and the sheer size and Grandeur of the stadium is something to behold so if you want to experience the passion and excitement of Brazilian football the matakana stadium is definitely the place to be number two ride the largest ferris wheel in Latin America the Rio yoop star Ferris wheel is an absolute architectural Marvel it stands at a town lowering 377 feet tall making it the tallest ferris wheel in all of Latin America just the sight of it is enough to take your breath away but the real fun starts when you get on the ride the gondolas are spacious and comfortable and the views from the top are absolutely breathtaking it's a great way to get a bird's eye view of Rio and take in the beauty of the city from above number one celebrate Rio Carnival the Rio Carnival is the biggest and wildest party on the planet and it takes place annually in the weeks leading up to Ash Wednesday it's a celebration of Brazilian culture music dance and all-around Good Vibes that draws millions of people from all over the world the main event is the parade at the sambadrome which features elaborately decorated floats and tens of thousands of costume dancers and musicians for weeks leading up to the main event the streets are filled with colorful floats costumed revelers and a live Samba music it's an unforgettable experience that you'll be talking about for years to come but the Rio Carnival involved isn't just about the party it's also an opportunity to immerse yourself in the vibrant culture of Rio you'll get to try delicious Brazilian food and meet new friends from all over the world click here for more tips while traveling in Brazil [Music]

8 Important Tips If You're Visiting Rio de Janeiro soon | Brazil

videos.transcript: in this video i'm gonna share a few tips with you guys for those who are thinking about planning a trip here to rio de janeiro reason being is that this city man it's a lot it can be your best friend or your worst enemy depending on the situation that you find yourself in however overall it's electric it's fun i mean it's yeah i'm enjoying this city but i'm gonna explain some of that in future videos let's go ahead and get into some of the tips that i have for you guys the first one is learning some basic phrases in the local language now i'm not saying you have to pay for a course or anything like that especially if you only plan a visit one time you know you're just coming here to market off your bucket list you don't plan on residing here permanently or anything like that however you should still learn some phrases such as greetings for example bom jia which means good morning bo taji which means good afternoon and then boa noichi which is good evening good night and then lastly obrigado which means thank you and then if you're a female you say obrigada with the a at the end and it's good to know these phrases because maybe where you're from people may not greet one another as much but here in brazil it happens a lot so if you walk into a place then you need help you first start off with a hey boa taj good afternoon and then proceed to say do you speak english you know i don't speak the local language as well versus going up to people hey do you speak english no hey do you speak english man hey do you speak english so learning just a few phrases a few greetings show some respect the next tip is transportation now just like many places in the world here you have ridesharing apps like uber there's also another popular one called 99 that you can download and use but keep in mind that there's also a metro here and works just fine it's not as complex as let's say the subway system in new york or tokyo here there are pretty much two ways north and south and it goes through all the major points in the city whether it be downtown near the museums near the lapa area it goes through uh neighborhoods like botafogo flamengo and then the tourist areas and copacabana the ipanema if you want to use the metro here you can purchase a card from one of the machines inside the stations or if your bank card has the feature where you can tap and pay you can use your credit card as well which is what i started using so just something i want to point out the third tip is probably one of the most important ones i want to stress and that is always having cash available because you can use your bank card pretty much anywhere in the city for the most part however it may not work in many places as well and i know this isn't just me because i've heard from other travelers who've had the same experience and i noticed a few things one there are certain machines that i have problems with one had like an orange top and then there was another one that had like a green and purple top so i noticed certain machines gave me a hard time another point i want to stress is that certain supermarkets they'll have signs that say they only accept debit card or cash and someone commented saying that's because to use the credit card service they get charged a fee so to avoid that they just don't offer it so the point i'm making is always have cash on hand now you don't have to bring the whole bank with you just a few of the larger bills just to cover yourself just in case when you're out and about the next tip on the list is something you cannot leave the country of brazil without now i know many people when they travel before going home they want to purchase souvenirs for their friends family and just as a memory of their time in the country one of those things again that you cannot leave brazil without purchasing is a conga this is the towel that the brazilians bring to the beach not the ones from the bathroom but this which is perfect because it's very thin so it dries off quickly the sand slides right off of it and it has many uses outside of the beach for example you can wear the conga around your body if you've ever been inside a plane or a bus you can use this as a blanket which i've used numerous times especially if it's super cold maybe you're on a budget airline and they're not offering any blankets or anything and it's just freezing inside just pull this outside of your bag and wrap it around you when i was in peru i went up a mountain rainbow mountain i used it as a scarf if you're at a hotel motel holiday inn or you book something on airbnb you get to your accommodation maybe the sheets aren't the greatest they're not the cleanest it's too late to switch rooms or book another accommodation you have to you have to wait till the next morning to maybe change to a different place you can use this conga as a blankie as a blanket and i'm sure there are other use cases but just overall this is definitely something that you can use time and time again next up on the list is where to stay now there are plenty of zones and neighborhoods you can reside in but one place i want to point out is copacabana it has many accommodations restaurants eateries however it's not the most modern if anything it can be a little a little gritty a little old so just keep that in mind for those who when they travel they prefer to stay in one of the be nicer areas and if you're looking for a place that's in the same zone right next door is an area called ipanema or obviously you're going to spin a little bit more and if you pass iponema you have a spot called leblon ipanema leblanc are good alternatives to copacabana if you're looking for an area with a little bit more money tip number six on the list is the food delivery apps now i know if you're here on vacation you don't plan on ordering food you plan on going out to eat going out to restaurants and watching the sunset views and taking in the vibe and atmosphere of this city however for those who are staying a little longer or maybe it's a rainy day you're a little lazy and you would prefer to order delivery a popular app is ubereats now i believe videos.title: 15 BEST Things to do in Rio de Janeiro 🇧🇷 What to do in Brazil | Travel Guide & Tips
videos.description:
What to do in Rio de Janeiro Brazil?
Lets discuss the 15 Best things for tourists to do in Rio.

Look like a true Carioca with Brasil's original sandal, Havaianas: https://amzn.to/40UKIAO 

From gorgeous beaches and soaring mountains to the vibrant culture and delicious food, there’s so much to experience in this one of a kind metropolis. 

Here’s a list of some of the best things to do in Brazil’s second most populous city, Rio de Janeiro
00:00 Overview 
00:35 Ride the Cable Car up Sugarloaf Mountain (Pao de Açúcar) in Guanabara Bay
Sugarloaf Mountain is one of Rio's most prominent landmarks. This centerpiece to Rio’s UNESCO World Heritage designation not only looks amazing from the ground, but also offers stunning views of the surrounding landscape from its nearly 400 meter peak.
01:12 Take the train to Christ The Redeemer Statue (Christo Redentor)
Christ The Redeemer is the world famous statue atop Corcovado in the Tijuca National Forest. It’s one of the 7 Wonders of the World and is probably the first thing people picture when they think of Rio de Janeiro. While you can reach the 710-meter summit a few ways, including a strenuous hike, the best option might be taking the Corcovado Rack Railway from Cosme Velho.
02:00 Visit the magnificent beaches: Copacabana, Ipanema, Leblon, Barra de Tijuca & more
Rio de Janeiro has some of the most stunning beaches in the world. Copacabana, Ipanema, Leblon & Barra da Tijuca and just a few of the many magnificent beaches. Play beach volleyball or foot-volley, enjoy the Atlantic waters or simply people watch as you soak up the sun. There are both roadside food vendors & folks walking up & down the beach selling shrimp skewers, corn, coconut water, caipirinhas & more, so you’ll find plenty to eat & drink at the more touristy beaches. 
02:47 Explore the sprawling Rio de Janeiro Botanical Garden (Jardim Botânico)
The enormous 350-acre garden is home to thousands of species of plants from all over the world, including orchids, cacti, fruit trees and over 900 types of palm trees. You can explore the Japanese garden, the Amazonian section, a bee sanctuary and the famous row of massive palm trees.
03:30 Hike some of Rio's majestic mountains like Dois Irmoas, Pedra da Gávea & Pedra Bonita
04:25 Visit the Lapa neighborhood's Selarón Steps (Escadaria Selarón) & Carioca Aquaduct.
04:59 View the cityscape from the observation deck at Mirante Dona Marta
05:22 Visit Flamengo Park (aka Aterro do Flamengo, Eduardo Gomes Park, and Aterro do Brigadeiro Eduardo Gomes)
Flamengo Park is one of the largest seaside parks in the world & is popular with both locals & tourists. The park has beaches, lakes, soccer fields, basketball courts, tennis courts and even a skatepark. It’s home to a variety of trees including coconut, mango & palm trees and is a favorite for birdwatchers. Flamengo Park is also where you’ll find Rio’s Modern Art Museum, Municipal Theater and Cultural Center.
05:56 Tour some of Rio's culturally enriching museums: Museum of Tomorrow, the National Historical Museum, Copocabana Fort, Rio’s Aerospace Museum, the Fevela Museum, the Brazilian National team museum among many others. (Museu do Amanhã, Museu Histórico Nacional. Forte de Copacabana / Museu Histórico do Exército, Museu Aeroespacial, Museu de Favela, Museu Seleção Brasileira - CBF)
06:19 Watch a match at Maracanã Stadium (Estádio Jornalista Mário Filho)
Maracana stadium famously hosted world cup and Olympic matches, as well al the local Rio football clubs Flamengo, Batafogo and Fluminense.
06:39 Partake in the Carnival festivities (Sambódromo, Sambadrome Marquês de Sapucaí)
07:24 Explore Tijuca National Park & Forest (Parque Nacional da Tijuca)
Tijuca is one of the largest urban forests in the world & contains several rivers, waterfalls, mountains and caves, along with hundreds of species of animals & over 1000 species of plants. 
07:52 Visit the Lagoon District (Lagoa Rodrigo de Freitas)
It’s one of the most expensive neighborhoods in South America.The Lagoon District is home to luxury hotels, restaurants, and shops. It is also home to a number of cultural attractions, like the Museum of Image and Sound and the Parque da Cidade.
08:30 Take a Favela Tour 
Favela tours in Rio de Janeiro are controversial. Some think they're exploitative and unethical, while others think they're a way to learn about the favelas and support the local community & economy. Some tours focus on the history and culture of the favelas, while others focus on the social and economic challenges locals face. If you're considering taking a favela tour, it's important to do research and choose a reputable tour operator, ideally a local. Not all favelas or favela tours are equal, but they all carry potential risks. That said, a proper favela tour can be a culturally enriching experience.
09:09 Take a helicopter tour

corcovado Mountain in the tzuka National Forest it's one of the Seven Wonders of the World and is probably the first thing people picture when they think of Rio De Janeiro while you can reach the 710 m their Summit a few ways including a somewhat strenuous hike the best option in my opinion is taking the corado rack railway from cosme valo the train leaves every 20 minutes but you must buy a ticket ahead of time especially during Peak tour season as the tickets do sell out the railway passes through neighborhoods and Lush Forest on its 20-minute ride to the top we recommend taking the earliest trains available because the summit does get pretty packed as the day goes on but the views and overall experience is well worth the effort number three visit the iconic beaches Rio de Janeiro has some of the most stunning beaches in the entire world kokabana iima Leblon and Baja dauka are just a few of the many magnificent peaches you can play beach volleyball or foot volley enjoy the Atlantic Waters or simply people watch as you soak up the sun there are both roadside food vendors and folks walking up and down the beach selling shrimp skewers corn coconut water karinas and more so you'll find plenty need to eat and drink at the more touristy beaches like Christ the Redeemer the beaches do get more crowded as the day goes on for those wanting to enjoy the water we recommend keeping your phone and wallet in a waterproof carrying case with a trusted friend on the beach or back in your hotel altogether to avoid losing any valuables number four explore Rio's massive Botanical Garden Rio's Jardine botanico is a peaceful and biodiverse Oasis in the heart of the bustling City the enormous 350 acre Garden is home to thousands of species of plants from all over the world including orchids cacti fruit trees and over 900 types of palm trees you can explore the Japanese garden the Amazonian section A bee sanctuary and the famous row of massive palm trees one of the most photographed and recognizable sections of the park the garden even has a few cafes and restaurants inside the serenity of Jardine botanico is a steep contrast from the cacophony of the city and definitely deserves a spot on your itinerary next on our list is to hike some of Rio's magical mountains deyos Pedro deavia and Pedro Bonito are a few of the famous forested mountains that you can hike for jaw-dropping views of The Marvelous City and its surroundings do earos translates to Two Brothers and are the Twin Peaks between hosia and vial they Overlook the gorgeous Leblon iima Waterfront Pedra davia is a famous 844 me monolith in the Baja deuka neighborhood that's popular for rock climbing and repelling the hike is considered challenging and while the heights here are not for the faint of heart the view is unmatched Pedro Bonita just Inland of Pedra gavia has impeccable views of the previously mentioned mountains forests and beaches and is a popular spot for paragliding any of these hikes are worth your time just bring plenty of water and watch your step as these mountains have sudden and unforgiving drops number six visit the lapa neighborhood lapa is in the center of Rio and is famous for its historical monuments and nightlife popular sites include the Celeron steps and 18th century Aqueduct the Celeron steps are covered with over 2,000 colorful tiles from 60 countries and it's a popular place for tourist to take pictures the double tier laa arches are part of the karaa aqueduct built in the mid 1700s to bring fresh water from the karaoke River to the population of the city laa is a lively neighborhood that offers a more authentic experience of Rio number Seven View the cityscape from Moran donam Marta Moran donam Marta is a Scenic Viewpoint in Rio's Santa Teresa neighborhood with stunning views of the city Christ the Redeemer and sugar love Mountain to get here you want to take a taxi or an Uber followed by a quick hike through the forest and up some stairs once you reach the top you'll be rewarded with stunning panoramic views eight visit flamango Park Flamingo Park is one of the largest Seaside parks in the world and is popular with both locals and tourists the park has beaches Lakes Soccer Fields basketball courts tennis courts and even a skate park it's home to a variety of trees including coconut mango and palm trees and is a favorite for bird watchers Flamingo Park is also where you'll find Rio's Modern Art Museum Municipal theater and Cultural Center this is a thriving cultural Hub that's both packed with activity and a location to relax next up tour Rio's magnificent Museum we've already mentioned the Modern Art Museum but Rio has tons of other amazing museums to check out like the Museum of Tomorrow the National Historical Museum the Copo Cabana Fort Rio's Aerospace Museum the flla museum and the Brazilian national team Museum among many others 10 visit madaka Stadium we're better to watch the beautiful game than in The Marvelous City metaka Stadium famously hosted World Cup and Olympic matches as well as the local Rio football clubs Flamingo BOGO and flumin it's a great place to watch A Match but you can also take a tour of the historic Stadium 11 join the carnival festivities Rio's Carnival is the biggest carnival in the world and it's one of the most famous and iconic events in Brazil it's celebrated annually in the days leading up to lent and it's a time for people to let loose and have fun carnival is known for its elaborate costumes colorful floats and Lively music the main event is a Samba parade which features Samba schools from all over Rio de Janeiro competing to put on the best show the parade is held at the sambad Drome a stadium that's specifically built for Carnival Rio's Carnival is a truly unique and Unforgettable experience it's a chance to immerse yourself in Brazilian culture and experience the joy and excitement of the carnival be sure to buy your parade tickets early because they do sell out quickly 12 explore tzuka National Park tzuka is one of the largest Earth Urban forest in the world and contains several Rivers waterfalls mountains and caves along with hundreds of species of animals and over a thousand species of plants tourists and locals enjoy hiking biking swimming and bird watching in the park and just a heads up it's suggested to take a guided tour of the park to protect yourself from pickpockets and other criminal activity 13 visit the Lagoon District the Lagoon District in Rio de Janeiro is known for its affluent residence beautiful scenery and vibrant nightlife it's one of the most expensive neighborhoods in all of South America the Lagoon district is home to a number of luxury hotels restaurants and shops it's also home to a number of cultural attractions like the Museum of image and Sound and Park to see D the Lagoon is a popular spot for swimming sunbathing and boating while nearby parks offer Recreation like tennis basketball biking pickle ball and soccer AKA football to most other countries or fball to the local care IAS 14 take a flla tour flla tours in Rio de Janeiro are somewhat controversial some believe they are exploitive and unethical While others think they're a great way to learn about the favelas and support the local community and economy there are several flla tours available in Rio deeo some focus on the history and culture of the faelas While others focus on the social and economic challenges locals face if you're considering taking a flla tour it's important to do your research and choose a reputable tour operator ideally a local be aware that not all favellas or FLL tours are equal but they all carry potential risks that said a proper flla tour can be a culturally enriching experience last up take a helicopter tour if you can swing the price a helicopter tour of Rio dejero will be an unforgettable experience helicopter tours are a great way to see the city's iconic landmarks such as Christ the Redeemer Sugarloaf Mountain and Copo Cabana Beach they also offer a unique perspective of the city's layout and its UNESCO acknowledged natural beauty helicopter tours are likely more expensive than other types of Tours but they undoubtedly provide amazing Bird's ey views and a memorable experience Rio de Janeiro is a vibrant in diversity with something to offer for everyone whether you're interested in history culture nature or simply enjoy having a good time you're sure to find something you love in Rio thanks for watching

videos.transcript: Rio de Janeiro is a very big city. It is amazing, but I have to be honest, you need to be smart here. Rio is not a war zone, but it is a complex place. To give you an idea about the size, Rio is more than 460 square miles. This means Rio is much bigger than Chicago or Madrid. Most tourists think everything is close, but it is not. Sometimes you will spend one hour in traffic, and in Rio, traffic is not just boring, it can also be a safety problem if you are in the wrong place at the wrong time. If you are coming to Rio, you probably have three big questions. First, which area is actually safe to stay? Second, how do I move around without ending up in a dangerous place by mistake? And third, is one week enough to see the real city? In this video, I will tell you the truth. No filters. I live here, so I will give you the real insider information. We will start with the airport and the dangerous roads. Then, we will talk about the neighbors. I will show you where the good spots are and which places are no-go zones. We will also talk about real prices and how to not get scammed. Chapter 1 The Arrival Most international flights arrive at Galeão International Airport. Listen to me, your safety starts at the moment you walk out of the terminal. After you pass immigration, you will go to the arrivals area. Be careful here, don't take the first taxi that someone offers you at the door. These guys are often waiting for fresh tourists to charge three times more. If you want to save money and stay safe, you have two options. Option 1, Uber. It works well, but at Galeão Airport, you must go to the departures floor upstairs to wait for your car. Option 2, the blue taxis, radio taxis. They are safer and have a fixed price. You pay at the desk inside the airport before you leave. It is the best choice if you have many bags and want zero stress. Now, let's talk about the road from the airport to your hotel. It is called Linha Vermelha. This is a very important highway, but I have to tell you the reality. It can be dangerous. You will see big gray walls on the side of the road. They are there to block the noise, but also to hide the favelas and to protect the cars from stray bullets, if there is a fight between the police and the gangs inside. Sometimes, criminals block the road to rob many cars at once. We call this a arraistão. It doesn't happen every week, but it is a real risk. This is why I created the Rio Safety Map. I marked the safe roads and the no -go zones that your GPS might try to take you through as a shortcut. If you want to move around Rio without the fear of taking a wrong turn into a conflict zone, the link for my custom map is in the description. It's the easiest way to have peace of mind. Once you are in the tourist areas, the metro is the best way to move. It is safe and has air conditioning. We have line 1 and line 4. They connect the downtown to the beach areas like Copacabana and Ipanema. One ticket costs 7.90 reais, about 1 .50 cents. You can just use your credit card at the gate. It's fast and you avoid the traffic jams on the streets where robberies are more common. Look for the pink car of the metro. Between 6 to 9 a.m. and 5 to 8 p.m., one car is only for women. Men, if you go inside during these hours, you will have problems with the woman and the police. Respect this rule. And what about the buses? My honest advice? Avoid the buses. The drivers drive like they are in a race. They are very crowded and they are the favorite place for pickpockets. If you are a tourist, stick to the metro or Uber. If you go to the downtown area, use the VLT. It is a modern trend and it is very safe. But pay attention, you must buy and validate your ticket at the station before you enter. If an inspector catches you without a ticket, the fine is very high. Now let's talk about where you are going to sleep. Choosing a neighborhood in Rio is like a game of chess. One wrong move and you can end up staying next to a dangerous area without knowing it. I've noticed that some people get nervous about choosing a specific hotel or have a very complex itinerary. If that is your case, I do offer a private one-on-one consultation to review everything for you personally. But for 90% of you, just following the rules of the neighborhood I'm about to show you will be enough to keep you safe. Now let's look at the iceberg of Rio's neighborhoods and find out where you should and definitely shouldn't book your stay. Now that you know how to leave the airport and move around, let's talk about the city itself. Rio is a huge place, but for a tourist, not all areas are the same. If you choose the wrong neighborhood, your vacation can become a nightmare in minutes. Chapter 2. The real map of Rio. I will be very honest with you about each area from the most famous to the ones you should avoid. This is the South Zone, Zona Sul. 90% of the tourists stay here. It is the most beautiful part of the city, but it is also where most pickpockets are waiting for you. Copacabana. This is the most famous beach in the world, but here's the truth. Copacabana is old and very chaotic. The reality, the buildings are from the 1950s and 60s and the streets are always full of people. Pros, you have many hotels, many restaurants and the metro is very close. It's very convenient. Cons, it's very busy at night, many homeless people and pivetes, young pickpockets, walk around looking for tourists who are not paying attention. Insider tip, never ever walk on the sand of Copacabana at night. Stay on the sidewalk where the kiosks and the lights are. If you go to the dark sand at night, you will probably get robbed. Ipanema and Leblon. These are much better than Copacabana. They are cleaner, safer and the vibe is more high class. Pros, this is the safe zone. It's perfect for watching the sunset at Pedra do Arpoador. The streets are better organized and there is more police. Cons, it's very expensive. A bottle of water or a beer here can cost double the price of other areas. Botafogo and Flamengo. I call these the insider neighborhoods. Many young travelers and digital nomads stay here. The secret, the hotels here are much cheaper than Ipanema. It is a great place to stay if you want to save money but still be close to the action. Botafogo has amazing bars and great view of the Pão de Açúcar. Important, you cannot swim in the beach in Botafogo. The water is dirty. Stay here for the price and the food, but take the metro to Ipanema if you want to go to the ocean. Next to the center, Centro and Lapa. The center is where the history of Rio is. During the day, it is safe to visit the museums and the old churches. But listen to me, after 6 pm and on weekends, the center becomes a ghost town. It is very dangerous. Criminals wait for tourists who get lost in the empty streets. Lapa is the party area. It's famous for the white arcas and the samba. It's a lot of fun, but it's also very rough. If you go to Lapa at night, go by Uber and leave by Uber. Do not walk alone in the small dark streets of Lapa. Stay where the crowd is and keep your wallet and cell phone in your front pocket. The north zone, Zona Norte. This is the real Rio, where the working class lives and where the Maracana Stadium is. My advice, do not stay here. It is very far from the beach and the crime rate is much higher. Unless you are going to a football match, there is no reason for a first-timer to be in the north zone. Finally, the west zone, Zona Oeste, specifically Barra da Tijuca. People call this the Miami of Rio. It has big malls and modern buildings. Pros, it's the safest area for a family. Cons, it's very far. It can take one hour to get to the Pão de Açúcar or the Cristo Redentor. Also, you cannot walk to places. You need a car for everything. If you want the classic Rio vibe, don't stay in Barra. Now I need to tell you about the most dangerous mistake tourists make in Rio, the GPS trap. In Rio, a rich street can be exactly next to a dangerous community controlled by armed gangs. If you use Google Maps or Waze, the app might try to find a shortcut to avoid traffic. Never follow a shortcut that takes you up to a hill in Rio. We have a rule. If the street is getting narrow and you are going up, stop and turn back. Many tourists have been shot because the GPS took them inside a favela controlled by gangs. This is a real risk. This is why I always tell people to check my safety map. I mark the specific areas that are GPS traps so you don't make this mistake. In a city like Rio, a wrong turn can change your life. You probably want to see a favela. It's a part of the city soul. But don't go alone and don't go with just any guide you find on the street. Some communities like Vidigal or Santa Marta are safer for tourists, but many others are in a constant state of war between gangs and the police. If you want to visit a favela, find a local guide who lives there. They know the rules. For example, in many favelas, you cannot take photos of people or set on corners. If you take a photo of a drug dealer by mistake, you'll have a very big problem. Here is my updated ranking for you to choose your hotel. Ipanema and Leblon. This is the gold standard. It is the best mix of beauty and safety, but it's also the most expensive area in Rio. Parra da Tijuca. This is the safest choice in the entire city. It is perfect if you are traveling with family or if you want maximum peace of mind. Just remember, it is far from the historical sites. Flamengo. A great alternative for a local vibe. It is usually quieter and cheaper than the beach areas, and it's safer for walking during the day than Botafogo right now. Copacabana. Good for convenience and the classic Rio experience, but you must be alert 24x7 because of the high number of pickpockets on the street. Botafogo. Amazing for bars and restaurants, but it is currently a hot spot for phone thefts. If you stay here, you must be 200 % alert. My final advice for this part. If you have the budget, stay in Ipanema or Leblon. If you want the highest level of safety and don't mind using Uber for everything, go to Parra da Tijuca. No matter where you choose, remember, in Rio, your safety is more important than saving a few dollars. Now let's move to the last part of your guide. Real prices. How to eat like a local and the survival rules for the street. Chapter 3. Eating like a local and real prices. Now let's talk about one of the best things in Rio. The food. But I'm not here to tell you about the expensive restaurants you see on Trip Advisor. I wanna show you how a real carioca eats and how you can save a lot of money by avoiding the gringo tax. In Rio, the most authentic food experience is at the lanchonete. These are almost every corner in Copacabana and Ipanema. They don't look fancy, but they are the soul of the city. The insider snack. You must try a joelho, a pastry with ham and cheese, or a coxinha. The price. A snack and a Guaravita should cost around 10 to 15 reais, about 2 or 3 dollars. If a place tries to charge you 50 reais for this, they think you are a sucker. Just say no and walk to the next corner. For lunch, you must look for Restaurante Aquilo. This is a genius invention. You serve yourself at a big buffet. Meat, salad, pasta, even sushi. And you pay for the weight of your plate. Why it's good? It's fast, healthy and you see exactly what you are eating. The real cost. In a good neighborhood, Aquilo lunch should cost between 40 to 60 reais, 8 to 12 dollars. Insider tip. Avoid the restaurants that don't have a scale. If they have a fixed price for tourists, it's always a bad deal. Now let's go to the beach. The beach economy in Rio has two levels. Level 1 is the kiosks on the sidewalk. They are beautiful, they have music, but they are expensive. A beer there can be 15 reais. Level 2 is the sand. On the sand, you will see people selling mate, iced tea and biscoito globo. This is the real Rio experience. A mate on the sand should be 8 to 10 reais. The chair scam. If you rent a chair and umbrella, the price is usually 20 reais each. Some barraqueiros try to charge tourists 50 reais for a chair. Don't pay it. Ask for the price before you sit down. If they are too expensive, just walk 10 meters to the next tent. Chapter 4. Street survival. The art of camouflage. This is the most important part of the entire video. I am going to teach you the survival etiquette of Rio. Most robberies happen because the tourist looks like a tourist. Your goal is become invisible to the criminals. Rule number 1. The smartphone. In Rio, your phone is the most dangerous thing you carry. It is a magnet for thieves on bicycles. The pocket rule. Your phone must stay in your pocket or your bag 100% of the time you are on the sidewalk. The safe zone. If you need to check Google Maps, call an Uber or send a text, you must go inside a place. Go into a pharmacy, a supermarket or a clothing store. Use your phone there. Put it back in your pocket and then walk out. Never use your phone while waiting for an Uber on the street. Wait for the car inside the building. Rule number 2. The camouflage. If you walk around with a big professional camera, an expensive watch or a gold jewelry, you are telling the criminals. I have money, come and take it. Dress down. Locals in Rio dress very simply. Wear a t-shirt, shorts and "havianas", flip -flops. The gold rule. Leave your wedding ring, your Rolex and your expensive sunglasses in the hotel safe. You don't need them to enjoy the beach. If you look poor or simple, you are much safer. Rule number 3. Night mobility. Rio is a different city after the sun goes down. A street that is safe at 2pm can be very dangerous at 10pm. Don't walk. Even if your destination is only 5 blocks away, if it's late at night, take an Uber. It costs 10 reais, 2 dollars and it saves you from a potential robbery in a dark corner. Rule number 4. Money and ATMs. Never use an ATM on the street or in a dark area. Criminals use schemers to steal your card in full, or they wait for you to walk out with the cash. Where to get cash? Only use ATMs inside big banks during business hours, or inside a high-end shopping mall or a big supermarket. They have security guards and cameras. I know this sounds like a lot of rules. You might be thinking, is Rio even worth it? The answer is yes. Rio is one of the most beautiful places on earth. The energy, the music and the people are incredible. But you cannot be a naive tourist here. You have to be an insider. In Rio, we have a saying, não dá mole. It means, don't be soft or don't show weakness. If you pay attention to your surroundings, if you keep your phone hidden and if you use common sense, you will have the best time of your life. You will see the Cristo Redentor, you will drink caipirinhas on the beach and you will go home with only good memories. I created this channel and the safety map because I want you to love my city as much as I do, but without the trauma of a bad experience. If you found this information useful, please subscribe and check the links in the description for my map and my private consultation. Rio is waiting for you. Be smart, stay safe and I will see you in the next video.
